quiz.title: Unit 10 Quiz 12, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Precipitation Reactions and Gravimetric Analysis
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Precipitation Reactions, Selecting Precipitating Agents, Ion Concentrations, Net Ionic Equations, Gravimetric Calculations

questions.question: A solution is known to contain dissolved Ag⁺ ions. Which of the following could be added to the solution to form a precipitate?
questions.explanation: Ag⁺ forms an insoluble precipitate with Cl⁻ (AgCl). NaCl provides Cl⁻ ions. Nitrates and acetates are generally soluble with silver.

questions.question: A waste-water sample contains Ba²⁺ ions. Which reagent would be most effective at removing these ions by precipitation?
questions.explanation: Ba²⁺ forms insoluble BaSO₄ with sulfate ions. Na₂SO₄ provides SO₄²⁻ ions. Chlorides, nitrates, and bromides of barium are soluble.

questions.question: To precipitate Ca²⁺ ions from a solution while keeping other ions dissolved, a technician should add:
questions.explanation: Ca²⁺ forms insoluble CaCO₃ with carbonate ions. Na₂CO₃ provides CO₃²⁻ ions. Nitrates and chlorides of calcium are soluble.

questions.question: Which of the following substances would cause Fe³⁺ ions to precipitate out of an aqueous solution?
questions.explanation: Fe³⁺ forms insoluble Fe(OH)₃ with hydroxide ions. KOH provides OH⁻ ions. Chlorides, nitrates, and perchlorates of iron(III) are soluble.

questions.question: Which of the following would not be useful if you wanted to precipitate a metal cation from a solution?
questions.explanation: LiNO₃ provides nitrate ions, and all nitrates are soluble. It would not precipitate any metal cation. The other reagents provide anions that form insoluble compounds with many metals.

questions.question: Which solution contains a higher concentration of chloride ions? 0.30 M MgCl₂ or 0.50 M NaCl
questions.explanation:
0.30 M MgCl₂ gives [Cl⁻] = 0.30 × 2 = 0.60 M
0.50 M NaCl gives [Cl⁻] = 0.50 M
MgCl₂ has the higher chloride ion concentration.

questions.question: Which solution contains a higher concentration of nitrate ions? 0.15 M Al(NO₃)₃ or 0.40 M KNO₃
questions.explanation:
0.15 M Al(NO₃)₃ gives [NO₃⁻] = 0.15 × 3 = 0.45 M
0.40 M KNO₃ gives [NO₃⁻] = 0.40 M
Al(NO₃)₃ has the higher nitrate ion concentration.

questions.question: Which solution contains a higher concentration of sodium ions? 0.20 M Na₂SO₄ or 0.40 M NaCl
questions.explanation:
0.20 M Na₂SO₄ gives [Na⁺] = 0.20 × 2 = 0.40 M
0.40 M NaCl gives [Na⁺] = 0.40 M
Both solutions have the same sodium ion concentration.

questions.question: Which of the following expressions best describes the term net ionic equation?
options: An equation where all reactants and products are ions
An equation listing all ions that are not spectator ions
An equation where soluble substances are written in dissociated form
An equation where only products are written in dissociated form
questions.explanation: A net ionic equation shows only the ions that actually participate in the reaction, with spectator ions removed. It lists all ions that are not spectator ions.

questions.question: When an excess of NaCl is added to 500 mL of an AgNO₃ solution of unknown concentration, 2.87 g of AgCl(s) (143.3 g/mol) is recovered. What was the molarity of the AgNO₃ solution?
questions.explanation:
Moles AgCl = 2.87 g / 143.3 g/mol = 0.0200 mol
AgNO₃ + NaCl → AgCl + NaNO₃ (1:1 ratio)
Moles AgNO₃ = 0.0200 mol
Volume = 500 mL = 0.500 L
Molarity = 0.0200 / 0.500 = 0.0400 M

questions.question: A 250 mL sample of Na₂SO₄ is treated with excess BaCl₂. The resulting BaSO₄ (233.4 g/mol) precipitate weighs 1.17 g. Calculate the molar concentration of the original sulfate solution.
questions.explanation:
Moles BaSO₄ = 1.17 g / 233.4 g/mol = 0.00501 mol
Na₂SO₄ + BaCl₂ → BaSO₄ + 2NaCl (1:1 ratio)
Moles Na₂SO₄ = 0.00501 mol
Volume = 250 mL = 0.250 L
Molarity = 0.00501 / 0.250 = 0.0200 M

questions.question: 150 mL of a KOH solution is reacted with excess MgCl₂. If 0.583 g of Mg(OH)₂(s) (58.3 g/mol) is collected, what was the molarity of the KOH?
questions.explanation:
Moles Mg(OH)₂ = 0.583 g / 58.3 g/mol = 0.0100 mol
2KOH + MgCl₂ → Mg(OH)₂ + 2KCl (ratio KOH:Mg(OH)₂ = 2:1)
Moles KOH = 0.0100 × 2 = 0.0200 mol
Volume = 150 mL = 0.150 L
Molarity = 0.0200 / 0.150 = 0.133 M

questions.question: What is the solubility product expression for tin(II) hydroxide, Sn(OH)₂?
questions.explanation:
Tin(II) hydroxide dissociates as: Sn(OH)₂(s) ⇌ Sn²⁺(aq) + 2OH⁻(aq)
The solubility product expression is Ksp = [Sn²⁺][OH⁻]², where the coefficient of OH⁻ becomes the exponent.

questions.question: Excess NaOH is added to 100 mL of an unknown AlCl₃ solution. 1.56 g of Al(OH)₃(s) (78.0 g/mol) is filtered out. Find the molarity of the AlCl₃.
questions.explanation:
Moles Al(OH)₃ = 1.56 g / 78.0 g/mol = 0.0200 mol
AlCl₃ + 3NaOH → Al(OH)₃ + 3NaCl (1:1 ratio AlCl₃:Al(OH)₃)
Moles AlCl₃ = 0.0200 mol
Volume = 100 mL = 0.100 L
Molarity = 0.0200 / 0.100 = 0.200 M

questions.question: A 2.0 L sample of CaCl₂ solution is reacted with excess Na₂CO₃. After filtering and drying, 5.00 g of CaCO₃(s) (100.1 g/mol) is obtained. What was the molar concentration of the CaCl₂?
questions.explanation:
Moles CaCO₃ = 5.00 g / 100.1 g/mol = 0.0500 mol
CaCl₂ + Na₂CO₃ → CaCO₃ + 2NaCl (1:1 ratio)
Moles CaCl₂ = 0.0500 mol
Volume = 2.0 L
Molarity = 0.0500 / 2.0 = 0.0250 M

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered precipitation reactions and gravimetric analysis! 🧪⚖️
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review solubility rules and stoichiometric calculations.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on mole ratios and converting mass to moles correctly.
scoreMessages.0: Please review precipitation reactions, solubility rules, and gravimetric calculations.
